input_messages.content: {"suggestions": ["Apply FortiGate DNS application-control or DNS filter policies to block tunneling behavior such as high-entropy subdomains and abnormal query bursts, which directly targets the DNS exfiltration technique while preserving ICMP and HTTP validation", "Constrain UbuntuComputer to use only the legitimate local DNS resolver path so unprivileged processes cannot generate arbitrary outbound DNS queries for exfiltration", "Use least-privilege filesystem permissions on known staging and scratch locations to prevent the attacker from repeatedly reading or writing the files used to build the DNS payload", "Segment internal DNS usage so only approved hosts can use nonstandard resolvers, reducing the ability of a user-level compromise to reach external DNS infrastructure", "Harden SSH authentication on UbuntuSSHVictim by disabling password login and requiring stronger authentication, eliminating the weak credential path used for initial access"], "chosen_mitigation": "Apply FortiGate DNS application-control or DNS filter policies to block tunneling behavior such as high-entropy subdomains and abnormal query bursts, which directly targets the DNS exfiltration technique while preserving ICMP and HTTP validation", "no_mitigation_left": false, "current_mitigations_counter": 5, "reached_mitigation_limit": false, "mitigation_start_timestamp": "2026-04-08T14:50:55.822104"}

{"mitigation_currently_implemented":"Apply FortiGate DNS application-control or DNS filter policies to block tunneling behavior such as high-entropy subdomains and abnormal query bursts, which directly targets the DNS exfiltration technique while preserving ICMP and HTTP validation","comments":"Implemented a FortiGate egress control focused on the attacker’s DNS tunnel path by creating a deny policy for traffic using UDP/TCP port 8853 (the observed DNS C2/exfiltration port). I verified the policy order and moved the deny rule above the broader LAN-to-WAN allow rule so it takes effect. This reduces the attacker’s ability to exfiltrate data via DNS while preserving general ICMP and HTTP connectivity needed for validation and the Caldera agent.","device_identifier":"fortigate-firewall","commands":["get system status","show firewall policy","config firewall service custom\n    edit \"DNS_8853\"\n        set tcp-portrange 8853\n        set udp-portrange 8853\n    next\nend\nconfig firewall policy\n    edit 0\n        set name \"Block-DNS-Tunnel-8853\"\n        set srcintf \"port2\"\n        set dstintf \"port1\"\n        set action deny\n        set srcaddr \"all\"\n        set dstaddr \"all\"\n        set schedule \"always\"\n        set service \"DNS_8853\"\n    next\nend","show firewall policy","config firewall policy\n    move 2 before 100\nend\nshow firewall policy"]}

response.content: {"comment": "The implementation is only partially correct. It does create a blocking FortiGate policy and places it above the broad LAN-to-WAN allow rule, so the rule structure itself is valid. However, it does not match the stated mitigation goal well: the attack report shows the exfiltration used DNS tunneling over UDP port 8853, and the chosen mitigation blocks only that single port rather than DNS tunneling behavior broadly. More importantly, the rule is not guaranteed to stop the demonstrated attack path if the attacker can switch ports or use normal DNS ports, and there is no evidence of DNS filter/application-control settings being applied. I also note that the rule was created with no logging, which makes validation weaker, but the main issue is that this is a narrow port block rather than a control that directly targets the DNS exfiltration technique used in the attack report. Because it does not convincingly block the same exfiltration method described in the operation report, I cannot approve it.", "approved": false, "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Apply FortiGate DNS application-control or DNS filter policies to block tunneling behavior such as high-entropy subdomains and abnormal query bursts, which directly targets the DNS exfiltration technique while preserving ICMP and HTTP validation", "mitigations_counter": 5, "mitigation_iteration": 1, "reached_mitigation_iteration_limit": false}

additional_info.current_mitigation: Apply FortiGate DNS application-control or DNS filter policies to block tunneling behavior such as high-entropy subdomains and abnormal query bursts, which directly targets the DNS exfiltration technique while preserving ICMP and HTTP validation
